entries.update: Hillary Clinton was deposed by the Republican-led House Oversight Committee on February 26 as part of an Epstein investigation, with Bill Clinton scheduled for deposition on February 27.

Hillary Clinton and Bill Clinton testified in closed-door sessions before the House Oversight Committee as part of its Epstein investigation, with Hillary Clinton stating she did not know Jeffrey Epstein and Bill Clinton testifying the day after. Neither has been accused of crimes related to Epstein.
The House Oversight Committee released video depositions of Bill and Hillary Clinton on Monday as part of its Jeffrey Epstein investigation. The depositions took place the week before their release.
The House Oversight Committee voted on a bipartisan basis to subpoena Attorney General Pam Bondi regarding the DOJ's handling of Jeffrey Epstein-related files.
The House Oversight Committee voted on a bipartisan basis to subpoena Attorney General Pam Bondi regarding the DOJ's handling of Jeffrey Epstein-related files.
The DOJ released previously withheld documents from the Epstein files, including FBI interview reports relating to allegations made against President Donald Trump by a woman.

Rep. James Comer and the House Oversight Committee are investigating the Epstein case. The federal government requested that New Mexico stop its investigation into Epstein's Zorro Ranch around 2019 during Trump's first term.
Richard Kahn, Jeffrey Epstein's accountant, testified before the House Oversight Committee; Epstein accumulated hundreds of millions of dollars over his career.

Tova Noel, a former prison guard at the Metropolitan Correctional Center who was on duty the night Jeffrey Epstein died, is scheduled to testify before the House Oversight Committee as part of an ongoing congressional investigation.

New documents are revealing Jeffrey Epstein's network of connections and relationships cultivated among powerful and elite circles in the original sex-trafficking investigation that originated in Florida.
Leon Black and his wife Debra purchased the property at 1111 Calle Vista Drive in Beverly Hills for $38 million in 2016, which was subsequently sold for $47 million.

Deputy Attorney General Todd Blanche dismissed suggestions that victims of convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein have been ignored by the Justice Department and defended the Trump administration's handling of the investigation.
Metropolitan Police chief Mark Rowley stated police are investigating whether an email from Mandelson to Epstein constitutes a criminal offence.

Democrats walked out of a House Oversight Committee briefing with Attorney General Pam Bondi regarding Epstein files; GOP Chairman James Comer characterized the walkout as premeditated, and Bondi stated she would 'follow the law' in response.
Wall Street titan Leon Black paid Jeffrey Epstein $170 million, which Black described as compensation for tax and estate work.
Leon Black paid Jeffrey Epstein $170 million, which Black described as payment for tax and estate work.
David A. Ross sought Jeffrey Epstein's help to build art museums and is now facing fallout related to that association.
Jeffrey Epstein's accountant Richard Khan and lawyer Darren Indyke testified before the House Oversight Committee, both stating the DOJ never interviewed them about Epstein's crimes.
Epstein survivors filed a class action lawsuit against the Trump administration and Google seeking at least $1,000 per survivor and court-ordered removal of their personal information by Google.
A man was interviewed on suspicion of sex trafficking in connection with the Al Fayed investigation, following earlier interviews with three women under caution.
Ramsey Elkholy introduced Jeffrey Epstein to women as young as 18 years old over nearly a decade, with correspondence documented in emails between them.
Jeffrey Epstein provided funding to scientists, and the lack of legal or public scrutiny in science philanthropy allowed him to cultivate relationships with scientists.
Epstein files indicate a 15-year-old girl met then-Prince Andrew at Buckingham Palace. Mandelson attempted to get access to 10 Downing Street for Epstein's goddaughter.
Attorney General Pam Bondi was fired by President Trump on Thursday; her handling of Epstein files was a point of congressional interest.
The Epstein files were released containing accusations against rich and powerful people, but the DOJ made no additional arrests, with legal experts citing lack of evidence.
Todd Blanche assumed the role of Interim Attorney General on Thursday, replacing Pam Bondi, and stated he wants to distance the Department of Justice from its work on Epstein files.

Virginia Giuffre, prior to her death, accused Jeffrey Epstein of trafficking her to Prince Andrew, brother of King Charles. The Giuffre family is urging King Charles to meet with Epstein survivors in the United States.
Jeffrey Epstein spent his final days of freedom in Paris, where he met with influential figures.
According to an FBI interview, Epstein had sex in a vehicle in a prison parking lot while on work release from a Florida jail approximately 20 years ago.

Bill Gates has agreed to appear before the House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform in June as part of its investigation into deceased sex offender Jeffrey Epstein. Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick is also scheduled to testify before the same House panel as part of the Epstein probe.

Attorney General Pam Bondi did not appear for a House Oversight Committee deposition subpoenaed in connection with its investigation into the Jeffrey Epstein files. The DOJ communicated that Bondi would not appear and informed the committee that its subpoena is essentially moot, though committee members maintain the subpoena remains valid.

The Department of Justice announced that Attorney General Pam Bondi would not testify before the House Oversight Committee, at least temporarily, despite having been subpoenaed as part of the committee's probe into the Jeffrey Epstein files. Separately, UK politician Peter Mandelson was reported to have attempted to obtain access to 10 Downing Street for a person described as Epstein's 'goddaughter.'

Melania Trump delivered a televised statement from the White House denying any connection to Jeffrey Epstein, stating 'I have never had any knowledge of Epstein's abuse of his victims,' 'I was never involved in any capacity,' 'I've never been friends with Epstein,' and 'I am not Epstein's victim.' She called on Congress to continue the investigation and hold hearings focused on Epstein's survivors, and declared that claims linking her to Epstein 'need to end today.'

On April 9, 2026, Melania Trump issued a rare public statement denying that Jeffrey Epstein introduced her to Donald Trump and calling on Congress to hold a hearing for Epstein survivors. Former Justice Department chief Pam Bondi refused to appear before a congressional Epstein hearing, with a subpoena for her testimony remaining in effect and Democrats criticizing her absence. Rep. Thomas Massie argued that bringing survivors back before Congress would be redundant and that the duty to act rests with federal prosecutors.

Melania Trump issued a rare public statement on Thursday denying any ties to Jeffrey Epstein, calling him 'disgraceful.' The statement was described as unexpected given her typically low public profile, and her adviser spoke publicly about what motivated her to issue it.

Melania Trump made a surprise public statement calling for testimony from Jeffrey Epstein victims. House Oversight Committee Chairman James Comer, a Republican from Kentucky, responded to her statement and confirmed that congressional hearings featuring Epstein victims will take place. Separately, Epstein survivors pushed back on the call for public testimony. The House Oversight Committee has been investigating allegations of sex trafficking connected to Epstein, who was a convicted sex offender and is deceased.

A federal judge dismissed Donald Trump's defamation lawsuit against the Wall Street Journal, which had centered on the outlet's reporting about a letter allegedly connected to Jeffrey Epstein's 50th birthday. The judge ruled that Trump failed to sufficiently allege 'actual malice,' the legal standard required for public figures in defamation cases.

The US and Iran are holding nuclear talks in Geneva aimed at resolving their nuclear dispute, with President Trump accompanying diplomatic efforts with military threats and a Middle East force buildup.
Secretary of State Marco Rubio stated progress has been made in negotiations with Iran after the U.S. sent a draft peace proposal to Tehran.
Following US-Israeli attacks on key infrastructure, Iranian hardliners and politicians are demanding withdrawal from the Nuclear Non-Proliferation Treaty.
Iranian President Pezeshkian released a letter to Americans on X hours before Trump's scheduled address on Iran operations.
Iran rejected a U.S. ceasefire deal and instead conveyed a 10-point counterproposal through Pakistani intermediaries. The proposal demanded an end to ongoing attacks and an end to sanctions.

Pakistan has been mediating backchannel talks between the United States and Iran, with U.S. Vice President JD Vance involved in the mediation efforts. Iran has sought indirect engagement through the U.S. vice president.
Iran's ambassador to Pakistan stated that an Iranian delegation is scheduled to arrive in Islamabad on Thursday.
Negotiations between the U.S. and Iran are being hosted in Islamabad as both sides seek a diplomatic off-ramp to the conflict.

High-stakes U.S.-Iran negotiations were set for Islamabad this weekend, with Vice President JD Vance leading the American delegation in talks aimed at ending the war and finalizing ceasefire terms. The White House described the talks as ongoing, while Iranian Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei stated through state TV that Iran does not want war and seeks compensation. Some Republicans in Congress moved to assert legislative authority over any lasting deal with Tehran, citing a 2015 Obama-era law. China's role in the negotiations was also under discussion, with NBC News correspondent Janis Mackey Frayer covering that dimension of the diplomacy.

Vice President JD Vance traveled to Islamabad, Pakistan, to lead U.S. talks with Iranian officials aimed at ending the ongoing conflict, stating he expects 'positive' negotiations and that President Trump provided 'pretty clear guidelines' for the discussions. Iran's Parliament Speaker Mohammad-Bagher Ghalibaf posted on X that Iran will not negotiate with the U.S. without a ceasefire in Lebanon and the release of blocked Iranian assets, adding that two mutually agreed-upon measures have yet to be implemented. A fragile ceasefire remains in place, though multiple hurdles continue to strain it.

US and Iranian delegations convened in Islamabad, Pakistan, for high-stakes nuclear and diplomatic talks, with Pakistan serving as host and mediator. Vice President JD Vance participated on the US side, and a US plane carrying the American team was confirmed to have landed in Islamabad by two Pakistani sources. A fragile ceasefire between the US and Iran is currently in place, with the Strait of Hormuz among the key issues under negotiation.

Vice President JD Vance traveled to Islamabad, Pakistan on Saturday for direct talks with Iranian officials, the first such direct U.S.-Iran negotiations since 1979. The White House confirmed the talks, with Pakistani Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif playing a mediating role in hosting the negotiations. A two-week ceasefire between the U.S. and Iran, described as fragile, remains in effect, and Iran is seeking sanctions relief as part of the discussions. Special envoy Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner participated in trilateral talks with Iranian officials, drawing criticism from Sen. Mark Kelly. The Strait of Hormuz remains a key factor in the negotiations. Separately, Saudi Arabia confirmed that Pakistan sent fighter jets to the kingdom under a mutual defense pact.
After approximately 21 hours of negotiations in Pakistan, Vice President JD Vance announced that no deal was reached between the U.S. and Iran, describing the outcome as 'bad news for Iran.'
Russia launched drone and missile strikes on Ukrainian cities and infrastructure near the time of scheduled US-Ukraine talks in Geneva.
A Russian-operated gas tanker sank in the Mediterranean Sea following a Ukrainian drone attack, which Russia attributed to Ukraine.
A Russian-flagged gas tanker sank in the Mediterranean Sea following a drone attack that Russia attributed to Ukraine.
Russian publication Izvestia reported that Ukraine peace talks have been paused in connection with the Iran war.
Ukraine reported hitting a Russian oil terminal at Primorsk and a refinery in Ufa on Monday.
Zelenskyy stated that Ukraine has evidence that Russia is supplying Iran with intelligence.
Russia launched a large drone attack on Ukrainian cities including Lviv, damaging a UNESCO World Heritage site and injuring multiple people.
Ukrainian President Zelenskiy stated that the US has linked security guarantees to Ukraine ceding Donbas territory.
Ukrainian President Zelenskiy stated that the US has linked security guarantees to Ukraine giving up Donbas, according to Reuters.
Zelensky visited Saudi Arabia and offered Ukraine's drone expertise.
Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky made a surprise visit to Saudi Arabia to offer Ukraine's drone defense expertise developed during the war against Russia to the U.S. and Gulf States.
Russia announced a ban on gasoline exports starting April 1.
Russian strikes on Ukraine killed 4 people, and US Secretary of State Marco Rubio expressed openness to diverting weapons to Kyiv.
Ukrainian drones damaged Russia's Ust-Luga port, causing a fire to break out.
President Zelenskyy stated in an NBC News interview that he hopes President Trump cares about Ukraine's future, noting that Trump administration priorities center on the United States.
Ukraine's president stated that allies asked him about reducing attacks on Russian energy as prices rise due to the Iran war.
Estonia's defence forces detected an air threat overnight.
Ukraine's president hosted EU leaders at an event marking the Bucha massacre anniversary while Ukraine's negotiator Umerov was in Turkey for talks.
A Russian military transport plane crashed in Crimea, killing 29 people, according to the Russian Defense Ministry.
A Russian military transport plane crashed in Crimea, killing 29 people. Officials cited possible technical malfunction.
Russia is recruiting students for its drone forces in Ukraine and offering large financial packages as part of recruitment efforts.
Ukrainian President Zelensky warned about Russia's intelligence sharing with Iran in an exclusive NewsNation interview and discussed Ukraine's assistance to the US in its conflict with Iran.
Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky warned in an exclusive interview about Russia's intelligence sharing with Iran and discussed how Ukraine is helping the US in its war against Iran.
Fuel leaks occurred at Russia's Primorsk oil facility and a fire broke out at the NORSI oil refinery in Nizhny Novgorod, according to Russian authorities.
Zelenskiy traveled to Syria and met with President Sharaa.
A child was rescued from rubble after a Russian strike collapsed a home in Odesa, killing at least three people.
The Iran war has driven shortages and price spikes across multiple consumer product categories beyond oil and gas, including beer cans, helium balloons, and mortgages.

Farmers warned that the Iran war and its associated costs would drive up their expenses and subsequently affect food prices, with the recently announced ceasefire not expected to immediately relieve those pressures.

Vladimir Putin announced a ceasefire coinciding with Orthodox Easter weekend, including April 12, and both Russia and Ukraine signaled agreement to the truce. President Zelensky stated that Ukraine will complete repairs to the Druzhba oil pipeline in spring and said peace talks to end the war could resume soon, though he expressed skepticism about any imminent breakthrough.

A 32-hour Easter truce was established between Ukraine and Russia, though both Ukrainians and Russians expressed doubt it would lead to lasting peace. Russian casualties are mounting, recruitment is falling, and territorial gains are dwindling, while Ukraine has been increasing drone production. President Zelensky traveled to the Middle East in March, securing a series of 10-year agreements with Gulf State leaders, and subsequently announced that Ukrainian interceptors had downed Iranian drones in the region, with 228 Ukrainian experts previously deployed there.

Russian drone strikes killed at least two people in the Ukrainian city of Odesa overnight into Saturday, according to local authorities. A Ukrainian drone attack in Russia's Kursk region injured three people, as reported by the regional governor. Separately, Ukraine and Russia exchanged 175 servicemen each ahead of an Easter ceasefire.
Culiacán in northern Sinaloa state is experiencing a significant surge in cartel-linked violence.
El Mencho, a top figure in Mexico's criminal underworld and associated with the Sinaloa Cartel, died.

Trump held the inaugural Shield of the Americas summit in Doral, Florida and announced a new military coalition aimed at eradicating drug cartels in the Western Hemisphere. Kristi Noem was appointed as Special Envoy for the Shield of the Americas after being removed as DHS Secretary.
The US Coast Guard intercepted a semi-submersible vessel in the Pacific Ocean carrying 17,600 pounds of cocaine.
The US military struck an alleged drug trafficking vessel in the Pacific, killing two people and leaving three survivors.
The DEA named a Colombian leader a priority target and prosecutors are probing ties between the Colombian leader and drug traffickers.
An undocumented MS-13 gang member from El Salvador was sentenced to 35 years in prison in Southern California for a murder connected to a 2019 gang arrest.
The Colombian navy shut down 30 drug labs, seized 2 tons of cocaine, and seized thousands of gallons of smuggled fuel.

Érick Valencia Salazar, a co-founder of the Jalisco New Generation drug cartel, was extradited to the United States from Mexico as part of a group of 29 alleged drug lords and subsequently pleaded guilty.
Iranian authorities are conducting mass arrests of protesters and reformists following a deadly crackdown on anti-government protests.
The Iranian national women's football team returned to Iran after several players withdrew asylum requests.
Iran executed three individuals arrested in connection with protests, as announced by Iran's judiciary.
Iran executed three men, including Saleh Mohammadi, a young member of Iran's national wrestling team; these were the first executions carried out over January protests in Iran.
A State Department spokesperson called Iran's executions 'barbaric'; the UN and activist groups report Iran's regime is using the death penalty to crush dissent.
Iran's judiciary reported that two men involved in January protests were executed.
Iran has carried out executions by hanging, including of teenagers.
Iran released a Japanese national on bail who had been jailed amid antigovernment protests. The released individual is believed to be Shinnosuke Kawashima, NHK's Tehran bureau chief.

The U.S. and Israel launched joint military operation 'Operation Epic Fury' against Iran, followed by Iranian retaliation with missile strikes against U.S. bases in the Middle East and toward Israel. Explosions were reported across multiple Gulf states, airspace closed in several countries, and Israeli defense systems intercepted Iranian missiles over Haifa.
Anthropic's AI technology was used by the US military in connection with strikes on Iran, creating a significant dispute between the Pentagon and Anthropic over the military's use of the AI system.
NATO defense systems intercepted and destroyed an Iranian missile headed toward Turkey on Wednesday; Turkey confirmed the shootdown.
The Senate voted on a war powers resolution seeking to constrain President Trump's military authority regarding Iran, though the measure faced significant obstacles to becoming law.
Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney called for de-escalation of the Iran conflict while expressing support for the strikes.
The Senate voted on a war powers resolution seeking to constrain President Trump's military authority regarding Iran, though the measure faced significant obstacles to becoming law.

Israel claims to have killed Iran's top security official Ali Larijani and Basij militia commander Gholamreza Soleimani in airstrikes as part of a U.S.-Israel joint military operation that began on February 28. These targeted killings follow Iran's Ayatollah Ali Khamenei's death earlier in the conflict.
The Iranian national women's football team returned to Iran after several players withdrew asylum requests prior to returning.
Putin told Tehran that Russia stands by Iran, according to Reuters.
Iran conducted strikes near an Israeli nuclear research center.
A missile strike hit an elementary school in Minab, southern Iran on February 28, killing dozens of children including Soheil Monazzah.
Iranian missiles struck Israel, with explosions and smoke observed in Israel.
An Iranian missile attack struck the Israeli city of Arad, with drone footage revealing damage captured days after the strike.
U.S. and Israeli airstrikes struck Tehran and Isfahan early morning, with black smoke rising over both cities.
Former U.S. Secretary of Defense Leon Panetta stated there is no clear end to the war in Iran.
President Trump announced 'major combat operations' against Iran on February 28 involving joint U.S.-Israeli strikes; Israel and Gulf states reported Iranian strikes.
The US sent its first shipment of oil to Greece in four years as European refiners seek alternatives to Middle Eastern crude.
Iran has implemented a strategy to impose economic pain on Gulf states to force them to pressure the United States into restraining its actions.
A majority of Israelis support the war with Iran, though many doubt it will solve long-term security problems and question their prime minister's assurances and motives.
Israeli measures at Hebron's Ibrahimi Mosque have intensified control, with policies resembling those applied at Al-Aqsa Mosque.
President Donald Trump announced 'major combat operations' against Iran on February 28, with joint U.S.-Israeli strikes described as massive occurring.
Rescuers searched for survivors following U.S.-Israeli strikes on Iranian homes; Iranian Red Crescent documented the damage.

A synagogue in Tehran was destroyed in U.S.-Israeli strikes, with Mehr News releasing video showing extensive damage to the site. The Israeli military confirmed bombing Iran on April 7, and video showed widespread destruction in Pardis in northern Iran. Reports indicated 15 people were killed across Iran overnight. In Israel, an Iranian missile strike in Ramat Hasharon damaged sidewalks and overturned a car.

A synagogue in Tehran was struck during a wave of overnight U.S.-Israeli attacks, which Iran condemned as systematic targeting of civilian sites. Academic institutions were among the other sites hit, with a top Iranian university stating the attacks targeted the country's scientific progress and AI capabilities. Verified videos showed additional attacks on bridges, steel plants, and pharmaceutical facilities across Iran.
Hundreds of vehicles drove through Istanbul in a convoy organized to condemn the United States and Israel.
Video footage showed an explosion in the sky above Erbil, located in the Kurdish region of northern Iraq.
Ayatollah Ali Khamenei died as a result of US and/or Israeli military action, with his son Mojtaba Khamenei emerging as a leading candidate to succeed him as Supreme Leader.
Ayatollah Ali Khamenei died as a result of US and/or Israeli military action, with his son Mojtaba Khamenei emerging as a leading candidate to succeed him as Supreme Leader.

President Masoud Pezeshkian apologized to Iran's neighboring Gulf states for Iranian retaliatory strikes in the region, generating criticism from hardliners within Iran. Pezeshkian rejected the U.S. demand for unconditional surrender.
Mojtaba Khamenei, 56, was named Iran's new supreme leader by the Assembly of Experts following his father Ayatollah Ali Khamenei's death in U.S.-Israeli military strikes during the conflict.

Mojtaba Khamenei, 56, was named Iran's new Supreme Leader by the Assembly of Experts, succeeding his father Ayatollah Ali Khamenei who was assassinated. The leadership transition occurred amid major crisis.
Mojtaba Khamenei was named Iran's new supreme leader, succeeding his father Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, who died.
Mojtaba Khamenei has been named as Iran's new supreme leader, succeeding his father Ali Khamenei in a dynastic transfer of power.

The US and Israel are conducting military operations against Iran with coalition partners including the UK participating in defensive operations in Iraq. The conflict is generating significant military costs and casualties across multiple sides.
Mojtaba Khamenei became Iran's new supreme leader, succeeding his father Ali Khamenei, and issued his first public statement vowing to continue fighting in the ongoing Iran conflict.
Mojtaba Khamenei issued his first public statement as Iran's new supreme leader during an active military conflict with the United States and Israel.
Iranian opposition leader Reza Pahlavi spoke at the 2026 CPAC conference, pledging to 'make Iran great again' and calling on the Trump administration to 'stay the course.'
Angry scenes and rifts over the war are opening in Iran, creating tense relationships in Iranian families.

Following President Trump's conditional threat to target Iran's energy infrastructure if the Strait of Hormuz was not reopened, Iranian government officials called on students, artists, and athletes to form human chains around the country's power plants beginning at 2 p.m. local time on Tuesday.
A U.S. submarine struck and sank an Iranian warship with a torpedo in the Indian Ocean, with Sri Lanka taking custody of Iranian crew survivors.
Trump claimed an unnamed former president praised his Iran strikes, but all four living former presidents denied making such a statement to him.

A poll shared exclusively with the New York Post addressed Republican opinions on President Trump's decision regarding Iran, amid reports of Republican divisions over the decision to go to war with Iran.
Iran's IRGC claimed attacks on Aluminium Bahrain and Emirates Global Aluminium in retaliation for US-Israeli attacks on Iranian infrastructure.
An attack at Al Araby network's office in Tehran injured at least 10 people, prompting Qatar's Al Araby network to condemn the attack.
Pakistan says it is set to host U.S.-Iran talks soon, though it is unclear if the talks would be direct or indirect, with neither Washington nor Tehran commenting on the talks.
Pakistan stated it will continue Iran-US mediation efforts despite obstacles.

Iran's longest bridge linking Tehran to Karaj was destroyed overnight, and Iranian missiles and drones struck Israel, Saudi Arabia, the UAE, and Kuwait's largest oil refinery, setting some refinery units on fire.
An international jet fuel shortage is occurring, driving up airfares, triggering thousands of flight cancellations, and causing airline costs to rise.
Trump claimed the U.S. armed Iranian dissidents via Kurds, and Kurdish groups denied the claim.
A second U.S. airman was reported missing following a fighter jet shoot-down in Iran last week.

An Iranian attack in Kuwait killed six U.S. service members, with survivors disputing the Pentagon's official account of the incident. The attack occurred in the context of Operation Epic Fury, which began in late February; a two-week U.S.-Iran ceasefire took effect on Tuesday. Former Vice President Mike Pence also made public statements about U.S. operations against Iran.

Army survivors of an Iranian attack on U.S. forces in Kuwait disputed the Pentagon's account of the incident, claiming their unit was unprepared to defend itself. A two-week ceasefire between the U.S. and Iran was in effect, while more than 70 Democrats in Congress called for President Trump's removal over his threats against Iran and handling of military operations there. An anonymous Iranian woman described conditions in Tehran to The Australian, including nightly explosions, celebrations, and widespread fear. President Trump cited European nations' unwillingness to back the U.S. in the Iran conflict as a reason to scale back or abandon NATO commitments.

Israel struck targets in Beirut's southern suburbs and other Lebanese areas, issuing evacuation orders to civilians in multiple locations. Large numbers of Lebanese civilians were displaced by the strikes and evacuation orders.
Israel struck Beirut's southern suburbs after issuing evacuation orders in Lebanon, directing military action against Hezbollah.
Israel struck Beirut's southern suburbs and issued evacuation orders in Lebanon ahead of military operations directed against Hezbollah.
Israel is conducting active military strikes in Beirut and southern Lebanon against Hezbollah.
Israeli forces fired on a Palestinian family in the occupied West Bank, killing four members with two survivors.
The IDF conducted limited ground operations in southern Lebanon targeting Hezbollah positions.
Israel launched limited ground operations in southern Lebanon targeting Hezbollah positions.
The United Arab Emirates briefly closed its airspace as Israel struck Lebanon and Tehran.
Israel carried out a missile strike on a building in Beirut, Lebanon's capital, captured in photographs by journalist Fadel Itani.
Israeli air attacks and ground operations in southern Lebanon have killed more than 1,000 people and displaced more than 1 million people according to Lebanese government figures.
Israeli air attacks and ground operations in southern Lebanon killed more than 1,000 people and displaced more than 1 million people according to Lebanese government figures.

Israel conducted strikes on bridges over the Litani River in southern Lebanon and ordered destruction of structures near the border as military operations against Hezbollah intensified. Iranian missiles struck towns near an Israeli nuclear site, injuring multiple people.
An Israeli strike hit a building near Roman ruins in the Lebanese city of Tyre.
Israel conducted airstrikes targeting Beirut suburbs during its war against Hezbollah, with smoke rising over the city skyline.
More than a million people have been displaced in Lebanon due to the Israel-Lebanon military conflict.
An Israeli strike targeted a family compound, resulting in the deaths of 11-year-old Jawad Younes and his uncle Ragheb, who were laid to rest on Saturday.
A Lebanese family in Beirut launched a mobile aid initiative to support people displaced by ongoing conflict.
The Houthis carried out an attack on Israel that demonstrated their willingness to carry out their threats.
Two UN peacekeepers were killed in an explosion in southern Lebanon, the second fatal incident within 24 hours occurring during Israel's expanding military operation against Hezbollah.
Three Indonesian peacekeepers were killed in Lebanon in two separate incidents; the UN condemned the deaths.
The Israeli Defense Ministry announced it is stopping all defense-related acquisition from France, replacing French procurement with domestic Israeli or allied country sources.
U.N. peacekeepers in south Lebanon were killed by a roadside bomb; UN Security Council members condemned attacks on UN peacekeepers and called for de-escalation between Israel and Hezbollah.
Israel announced plans to create a security zone inside Lebanon with home demolitions, stating residents cannot return until northern Israeli safety is restored.
Israeli strikes killed 7 people in southern Lebanon as Israel seeks to establish a security zone and dismantle Hezbollah.
Israeli strikes killed 7 people in southern Lebanon as Israel seeks to establish a security zone and dismantle Hezbollah.
Israel has conducted attacks on hospitals and medical centres in southern Lebanon, targeting healthcare workers and contributing to displacement and a health crisis.
Israel is establishing a buffer zone in southern Lebanon through bombing that forced residents from leveled homes and destroyed bridges over the Litani River.

Lebanon reports at least 54 health workers are among more than 1,400 people killed by Israel during the current invasion, with human rights groups claiming first responders are being targeted while Israel denies this.
Christian Lebanese families were displaced during Easter celebrations due to the military conflict.
Up to 1.2 million people were forced to flee Lebanon due to Israeli attacks, with many experiencing displacement for a second time in recent months.

Israeli forces raided the Muslim Youth Association in Hebron, firing tear gas at Palestinians during the operation. In Lebanon, firefighters responded to a fire in the southern city of Maarakeh following an Israeli strike in the area.

More than one million people in Lebanon have been displaced as a result of Israel's ground operation there, constituting a significant humanitarian crisis. Separately, a shooting incident occurred outside the Israeli Consulate in Istanbul, Turkey, in which Turkish police engaged suspects in a gunfight, killing at least one and injuring at least two others.

Israel conducted what it described as its largest military strikes against Hezbollah in Lebanon on Wednesday, hours after the US-Iran ceasefire was announced, hitting central Beirut without warning. Trump confirmed Lebanon was not included in the ceasefire agreement, and Israel stated the deal did not apply to its operations against Hezbollah. The Lebanese health ministry reported 89 people killed and 700 wounded. Israel's Foreign Ministry sent a warning to Lebanon's prime minister and president instructing them not to interfere with Israeli operations against Hezbollah.

Israel launched major military strikes on Hezbollah positions in Lebanon shortly after a US-Iran ceasefire was announced, killing at least 182 people and injuring 890, according to Lebanon's health ministry, with widespread destruction reported in Beirut. Israel stated the ceasefire agreement does not apply to its operations in Lebanon, while Iran claimed the strikes constituted a ceasefire violation, placing the fragile agreement under significant strain.

Israel continued striking targets in Lebanon following Tuesday's U.S.-Iran ceasefire, with a dispute persisting between Israeli and White House officials on one side and Pakistani brokers and Iran on the other over whether the ceasefire covers Lebanon and Hezbollah. President Trump confirmed he asked Netanyahu to scale back the Lebanon strikes. In Gaza, Al Jazeera journalist Mohammed Wishah was killed in an Israeli strike on Wednesday, with a funeral held shortly after; separately, Israel bombed Gaza on 36 of the past 40 days, killing 107 people, permitting only 8 percent of medical evacuations, and admitting 20 percent of requested aid trucks.

Over 72,000 people have been killed and 172,000 injured in Gaza, with Israeli attacks reported during a US-brokered ceasefire that has been in place for six months, characterized as violations of the truce. Palestinian journalist Muath Amarne, held in Israeli detention, reported that his prosthetic eye fell out after an infection while in custody. Restrictions on the Rafah crossing have also affected Palestinian access to education.

Israel continued military strikes in Lebanon, placing the ceasefire under serious strain; Iran has conditioned its participation in U.S.-Iran negotiations on a Lebanon ceasefire being in place. Israel approved 34 new West Bank settlements, which the Palestinian Presidency condemned as a 'flagrant violation of international law.' In Gaza, approximately 10,000 Palestinians remain missing and are believed buried under rubble from Israel's military campaign, while aid delivery remains insufficient and families report being unable to bury their dead six months into a ceasefire. A Trump administration peace board working on a Gaza plan is facing a cash shortage that is stalling progress, and Prime Minister Netanyahu requested a delay in his corruption trial testimony.

An Israeli airstrike on a government building in Nabatieh, southern Lebanon, killed at least 13 people, including Lebanese officers. Hezbollah fired a barrage of rockets at northern Israel, damaging a 1,500-year-old Byzantine church in Nahariya. In Gaza, at least seven Palestinians were killed in Israeli attacks, including an early-morning strike on civilians in the Bureij camp and a drone attack near a mosque in central Gaza that killed at least six people. Israeli attacks in Beirut this week also resulted in deaths among residents.

Lebanon held funeral ceremonies for 13 members of its State Security agency killed in an Israeli strike. Overnight Israeli air strikes in Gaza killed seven people, with families gathering over bodies outside a Gaza hospital in the aftermath. Iran's foreign ministry stated that Iran is in contact with Lebanon and working to ensure ceasefire commitments are respected.

Israeli and Lebanese officials met in Washington, D.C. on Tuesday for direct diplomatic talks — the first between the two countries in over 30 years, with the previous direct discussions having taken place in 1993.
A U.S. submarine sank an Iranian warship with a torpedo strike in the Indian Ocean, and Sri Lanka took custody of Iranian crew members.
Russia provided intelligence to Iran regarding locations of US military forces, troop positions, aircraft, and ships in the Middle East, according to multiple unnamed sources.
Six U.S. service members were killed in connection with Operation Epic Fury. President Trump attended a dignified transfer ceremony for the fallen service members.
The Iran conflict caused significant disruption to global energy markets, with economic fallout disproportionately affecting energy-importing nations in Asia and Europe.

Trump threatened Iran with significantly increased military strikes if it disrupts oil flow through the Strait of Hormuz, referencing strikes 'much harder than previous attacks' amid ongoing active military conflict.

A deadly blast struck an Iranian school in southern Iran, killing a large number of people including children, with video and other evidence increasingly pointing to likely US involvement using a Tomahawk missile.
Spain withdrew its ambassador from Israel in protest over the war.

Active military conflict between the US and Iran, referred to as Operation Epic Fury, is underway with no clear exit strategy. Trump demanded Iran's 'unconditional surrender' as a condition for ending hostilities, while US threats to target Iran's power infrastructure contributed to escalation.
A French soldier was killed and other French soldiers were wounded in a drone attack in Iraq, where French forces are present to combat ISIS.

Oil prices surged due to the US-Israeli conflict with Iran, prompting the Trump administration to temporarily ease Russian oil sanctions for 30 days. Ukraine's President Zelensky and European allies criticized the sanctions relief, while rising energy prices have increased consumer costs and negatively affected US stock markets.
The Iran conflict is causing significant disruption to global energy supplies and substantial increases in oil prices, placing financial pressure on consumers and households.
Energy Secretary Chris Wright predicted the conflict with Iran would end within weeks and characterized its energy price impact as short-term, though disruptions have already elevated gas prices.

US and Israeli strikes on Iran began February 28, entering a third week of conflict. The Strait of Hormuz is blocked or unsafe for commercial shipping, disrupting global energy markets. US gasoline and oil prices have risen sharply, 200 US troops have been wounded, and a US KC-135 refueling plane crashed in Iraq killing 6 airmen.
Ali Larijani, secretary of Iran's Supreme National Security Council, was killed in an Israeli airstrike. Israel claimed responsibility and Iran confirmed his death.
Israeli officials stated that the US knew of the strike on an Iranian gas field in advance.
Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth has made statements connecting U.S. military actions to Christian moral justifications.

The US-Iran war entered its fourth week with joint US-Israel strikes on February 28; Iran fired ballistic missiles at the US-UK base at Diego Garcia, Iran's Natanz nuclear enrichment facility was struck, and the Strait of Hormuz closure triggered a global energy crisis. President Trump announced he is considering winding down US military efforts while the Pentagon sought $200 billion in additional funds and the US deployed additional Marines and warships to the region. The UK allowed US use of its bases for strikes on Iranian military assets threatening the Strait of Hormuz, and Iran threatened UK bases in response.
China urged the United States and Israel to stop military action in the Middle East and warned of a 'vicious cycle' from continued military action.

An Iranian-linked terrorist group claimed responsibility for an arson attack in Antwerp, Belgium on Monday night, in which a car in the Jewish community was set on fire shortly after midnight; two suspects were apprehended and no people were injured.
Trump postponed potential strikes on Iranian power plants, citing ongoing constructive talks as the reason.
The Pentagon is reportedly preparing military options related to Iran as the Trump administration considers endgame scenarios.
Senator Lisa Murkowski of Alaska is drafting a formal authorization for military force against Iran as the Trump administration has excluded Congress from the operation.
Iran conducted a sophisticated information war with propaganda and disinformation flooding the internet to undermine support for U.S. and Israeli attacks.
Ammonia prices increased approximately 20% and urea prices increased approximately 50% since the start of the Iran war due to disruptions in fertilizer ingredient supply.
White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt hosted a news briefing at 1 p.m. amid a Department of Homeland Security funding battle and the Iran war.
Officials and government supporters took to streets marking Islamic Republic Day while the U.S. and Israel continued bombing operations.
Pakistan stated it will continue Iran-US mediation efforts despite obstacles as Trump threatened to bomb Iran 'back to the Stone Ages'.
U.S. intelligence reports Iran is rapidly repairing missile bunkers as destroying Iran's missile capability remains a key U.S. goal.

An F-15E fighter jet was shot down over Iran and a U.S. crew member was rescued in a subsequent operation deep inside Iranian territory. Iran rejected a Trump administration 48-hour ultimatum regarding the Strait of Hormuz. Iran conducted drone attacks on Kuwait's infrastructure including oil, power, and government sites.
Iran requested FIFA relocate its World Cup games to Mexico and awaits FIFA's response, with Iran's World Cup participation dependent on relocation from the U.S.
Iranian Fars News Agency reported that two C-130 aircraft and two UH-60 Black Hawk helicopters were destroyed during the U.S. rescue mission in Iran.
U.S. forces conducted a rescue mission on Easter morning to recover a downed pilot from Iranian territory.

President Trump confirmed that a U.S. F-15 fighter jet was brought down in Iran by a shoulder-fired missile, and that two airmen were subsequently rescued. Trump described the rescue mission as one of the most complex the U.S. military has ever attempted. Israeli journalist Amit Segal identified himself as the first to report the missing U.S. airman.

The United States conducted strikes on military targets on Iran's Kharg Island, a critical component of Iran's oil economy, ahead of Trump's Tuesday deadline. Pope Leo XIV condemned Trump's threats against Iran's civilization as 'truly unacceptable.' An investigation is underway to identify who leaked information to the press about a U.S. airman missing in Iran after a fighter jet was shot down on Good Friday.
A CIA tool called 'Ghost Murmur' was linked to the rescue of a US airman in Iran last weekend. The airman was injured at the time of the rescue.

US and Israeli bombardment has caused widespread destruction in Iran, and Iran's government is pursuing a peace agreement, with sanctions relief among the considerations in negotiations. US intelligence assessed that China is preparing a weapons shipment to Iran, as reported by CNN. India, heavily dependent on Gulf oil imports, is experiencing shortages as a consequence of the ongoing conflict in the region.

Pope Leo XIV issued a condemnation of the U.S.-Iran war during an evening prayer service at St. Peter's Basilica on Saturday, characterizing the conflict as a 'demonic cycle of evil.' Leo had initially limited early comments to muted appeals for peace before stepping up criticism starting on Palm Sunday. An Iranian rabbi reported that a synagogue in Tehran — one of the city's few remaining synagogues — was destroyed in an Israeli strike. American officials also reported that China may have shipped missiles to Iran and that Beijing is allowing some companies to sell Tehran supplies usable in military production.
Pope Leo XIV used the phrase 'delusion of omnipotence' to describe what is driving the war during a vigil at St. Peter's Basilica, again calling for peace in connection with the conflict.

A US-Israeli military offensive against Iran began on February 27 and has disrupted shipping through the Strait of Hormuz, which handles approximately 20% of the world's oil supply. Iran is signaling intent to deploy mines in the critical shipping route, and shipping traffic has declined since the conflict began.
US forces destroyed 16 Iranian mine-laying vessels near the Strait of Hormuz. Iran has threatened to shut down the strait, a critical shipping corridor carrying roughly 20% of the world's oil.
Three ships were attacked near the Strait of Hormuz on Wednesday; Iran is linked to the attacks, which raised concerns about global oil exports and regional economic stability.
Oil and fuel prices have surged significantly, prompting the US to tap the Strategic Petroleum Reserve as part of an emergency response.

A US-Israeli military conflict with Iran has disrupted global energy markets, causing significant rises in oil and energy prices. Emergency measures are being considered to address energy price impacts.
Oil prices rose above $100 per barrel driven by military strikes on Middle East energy infrastructure amid the regional conflict.

Trump appealed to allied nations including NATO members and China to help secure or reopen the Strait of Hormuz, but allied nations have responded unenthusiastically or rebuffed the request for military support.
Trump called on other nations to help secure the Strait of Hormuz but later stated the US does not need assistance. European allies have been reluctant or unwilling to join the coalition.
U.S. Treasury Secretary stated that the United States is allowing Iranian ships to pass through the Strait of Hormuz.
A US Treasury Secretary made a public statement regarding Iranian ships passing through the Strait of Hormuz.
The Israel-Iran conflict has disrupted the Strait of Hormuz and caused oil and energy prices to rise, with broader supply chain and consumer price impacts reported.

Trump and UK Prime Minister Starmer agreed during a phone call that the Strait of Hormuz must reopen. Trump issued a 48-hour ultimatum to Iran threatening further US strikes on Iranian energy infrastructure.
President Trump announced that Iran allowed eight oil tankers through the Strait of Hormuz, stating Iranian negotiators informed the U.S. of this decision during ongoing negotiations.
UK ministers announced that sanctioned vessels can now be boarded by UK armed forces, prior to a sanctioned oil tanker entering UK waters.
Iran enacted a blockade of the Strait of Hormuz, impacting oil prices and affecting downstream industries including fertilizer.
Two LPG tankers bound for India cleared the Strait of Hormuz, as reported by the government.
A Canadian activist captured footage of dozens of oil tankers and cargo ships in the vital Hormuz Strait waterway.
Secretary of State Marco Rubio told Al Jazeera that the U.S. will not accept Iran's claim over the Strait of Hormuz.
Bahrain circulated a revised UN draft regarding the Hormuz Strait that drops binding enforcement provisions.
A majority of recent ship traffic through the Strait of Hormuz is linked to Iran. Trump claimed Iran allowed eight oil tankers through the strait.
Two ships passed through the Strait of Hormuz and arrived at Karachi Port.
Two ships passed through the Strait of Hormuz and arrived at Karachi Port.
President Trump dismissed concerns about the Strait of Hormuz in his Wednesday night address to the nation and asserted the U.S. produces sufficient oil.
Japanese, French, and Omani vessels crossed the Strait of Hormuz.

Rep. Mike Turner stated Iran's military capabilities are being significantly diminished and expressed doubt that ground troops will be necessary to reopen the Strait of Hormuz, while Trump set a Tuesday deadline for Iran to open the Strait or face consequences.
Iraq urged its oil customers to send oil loading plans following a Hormuz exemption.
Libya's oil sector disputes and Hormuz tensions are creating energy supply concerns for Europe.

Trump set a Tuesday deadline for Iran to reopen the Strait of Hormuz, threatening military strikes on Iranian infrastructure including power plants and bridges. Iran adopted a defiant stance and mobilized youth to form human chains around power plants as shields against threatened strikes. Saudi Arabia intercepted 7 missiles, with debris falling near energy facilities. A group of OPEC+ members agreed on Sunday to increase output by 206,000 barrels per day once the Strait reopens, with implementation planned for May. Asian airlines began trimming schedules and carrying extra fuel as supplies tightened amid the escalating tensions.

President Trump set an 8 p.m. EDT Tuesday deadline for Iran to agree to a peace deal and reopen the Strait of Hormuz, threatening 'complete demolition' of Iranian power plants and bridges and posting on Truth Social that 'a whole civilization will die tonight.' The U.S. military struck targets on Iran's Kharg Island, and the US Embassy issued a warning for Americans in Iran to evacuate or shelter in place. Russia and China vetoed a UN Security Council resolution aimed at reopening the strait, despite 11 of 15 members voting in favor and the resolution having been watered down ahead of the vote. Oil prices rose substantially and U.S. stock markets fell in response to the escalating tensions, with airlines including Delta raising checked baggage fees and ticket prices due to surging jet fuel costs. Iranian officials called on citizens to form human chains around power plants and bridges targeted in U.S. threats, while Iran's UN ambassador condemned Trump's social media post as 'deeply irresponsible.' Pakistan's Prime Minister called on Trump to extend the deadline by two weeks, VP JD Vance urged Iran to negotiate a ceasefire, and over 30 congressional Democrats called for Trump's removal via the 25th Amendment.

Trump announced a two-week ceasefire with Iran on Truth Social shortly before his stated deadline, conditional on Iran reopening the Strait of Hormuz. Tehran confirmed it agreed to guarantee passage through the strait for two weeks, contingent on a halt to attacks against Iran. Pakistan's Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif played a key diplomatic intermediary role, requesting Trump extend his deadline by two weeks to allow for further diplomacy. Oil prices fell and U.S. stock futures rose following the announcement, while Israel backed the ceasefire but stated it did not extend to Lebanon, where Israeli strikes continued. Maersk expressed caution regarding Hormuz shipping, and JPMorgan had warned gas prices could hit $5 per gallon if the strait remained closed. The UK pledged to support the ceasefire and Prime Minister Starmer announced plans to visit the Middle East, while crowds in Tehran burned U.S. and Israeli flags.

President Trump announced a two-week US-Iran ceasefire less than 90 minutes before an 8 p.m. Tuesday deadline, declaring it a 'total and complete victory' for the United States. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth, alongside Joint Chiefs Chairman Dan Caine, held a Pentagon briefing declaring the military operation—named 'Operation Epic Fury'—a decisive victory, claiming Iran accepted the ceasefire 'under overwhelming pressure.' Pakistan played a key diplomatic role in brokering the deal, with Vice President JD Vance confirmed to lead the US negotiating team for follow-on talks in Islamabad; Turkish intelligence was also reported to have played a role. Stock markets rose sharply and oil prices fell following the announcement, though Iran subsequently closed the Strait of Hormuz citing Israeli strikes on Lebanon, and the ceasefire's durability remained uncertain. Iran presented a 10-point peace plan asserting its right to enrich uranium and demanding sanctions relief, while Trump insisted Iran would not be permitted to enrich uranium; Trump also announced 50% tariffs effective immediately on any country supplying military weapons to Iran. Senator Lindsey Graham described the deal as having 'troubling aspects,' Israeli opposition leader Yair Lapid called it a 'political disaster,' and Vice President Vance characterized it as a 'fragile truce' contingent on Iran negotiating in good faith.

A US-Iran ceasefire was announced, sending the Dow up more than 1,000 points and causing oil prices to plunge 16% — the largest single-day drop since 2020 — as part of a deal that includes the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz. However, the ceasefire was immediately strained: no oil or gas tankers transited the Strait in the first 24 hours, traders remained wary about restricted traffic, and Goldman Sachs warned crude could reach $115 by year's end. Israel launched major strikes on Lebanon shortly after the announcement, with Iran claiming this violated the ceasefire agreement and the US and Iran disputing whether Lebanon was covered; VP JD Vance characterized the disagreement as a 'legitimate misunderstanding.' Trump announced US forces deployed under Operation Epic Fury would remain in and around Iran until a permanent agreement is reached, threatened 50% tariffs on countries supplying Iran with weapons, and lashed out at European partners and NATO for not contributing militarily — NATO chief Mark Rutte met with Trump and Rubio at the White House in what he described as a 'very frank' meeting. Iran publicly released a 10-point proposal related to negotiations, which Trump dismissed on Truth Social as a 'fake ten point plan,' attacking CNN and the New York Times for their coverage. The LNG industry was described as 'scarred' by the conflict, GOP members expressed concern about Iran-related political fallout ahead of midterms, and Financial Times commentator Robert Armstrong applied his 'TACO trade' framing — 'Trump Always Chickens Out' — to Trump's abrupt policy reversal.

A two-week U.S.-Iran ceasefire took effect on Tuesday, brokered by Pakistani officials, though the Strait of Hormuz remained effectively closed with only a handful of ships transiting since the announcement, and Iran announced plans to tax oil-carrying vessels at a rate equivalent to $1 per barrel in cryptocurrency. Israel struck over 100 Hezbollah targets in Lebanon in the hours following the ceasefire, killing hundreds, prompting Iran to warn of repercussions and dispute the White House and Israel's position that Lebanon was not covered by the agreement. President Trump posted late Wednesday threatening 'bigger, and better' military strikes against Iran if a deal is not reached or complied with, while also telling Netanyahu to scale back the Lebanon strikes; Trump told NBC News on Thursday he was 'very optimistic' about a permanent peace deal. Vice President JD Vance is set to lead a U.S. delegation in talks this weekend, while U.S. troops remain deployed in the region pending Iranian compliance. Markets reflected ongoing uncertainty — oil prices rebounded Thursday before trimming gains, European and African crude hit record levels, the national average gas price reached $4.17 per gallon, and Federal Reserve officials said the conflict raised the odds of a rate cut. House Republicans blocked a Democratic resolution to limit Trump's Iran war powers during a pro forma session, and NATO Secretary-General Mark Rutte publicly acknowledged that allies were slow to support the U.S., while discussing potential NATO involvement around the Strait of Hormuz.

President Trump posted a warning to Iran on Truth Social on Thursday, accusing Iran of doing a 'very poor job' of allowing oil through the Strait of Hormuz and stating Iran was not living up to their agreement. A two-week US-Iran ceasefire was in place, but Iran continued to maintain significant control over Strait of Hormuz traffic, through which one-fifth of the world's seaborne oil passes. The national average price for regular gas stood at $4.153 per gallon on Friday per AAA, marking the first one-cent decrease since the ceasefire announcement, after prices had risen since February. The war with Iran was identified as a key factor driving an expected surge in U.S. consumer inflation in March 2026, with higher fuel costs also raising food and travel prices. Inside Congress, calls for investigations into prediction market platform Polymarket increased after anonymous traders made well-timed bets on major geopolitical events, including the Iran war, hours before those events occurred.

President Trump publicly condemned Iran's plans to charge fees to oil-carrying vessels traversing the Strait of Hormuz, one day after Trump himself had proposed a 'joint venture' involving similar tolls. A European airport trade body warned that if the Strait of Hormuz did not reopen within three weeks, the EU airline industry could face fuel shortages. The Bureau of Labor Statistics reported that U.S. inflation rose to 3.3% year-over-year in March — the highest level in nearly two years — driven primarily by a large monthly surge in gasoline prices, with the Iran war cited as the key geopolitical factor behind rising energy costs. Consumer prices rose 0.9% month-over-month in March, and consumer sentiment fell to record lows in April, also attributed to the Iran conflict.

U.S. Central Command announced mine-clearing operations in the Strait of Hormuz, with two U.S. naval ships transiting the waterway as part of the effort. Iran issued a threat in response to the U.S. warship transit, while Chinese oil tankers also transited the strait. President Trump stated that U.S. forces are clearing the Strait of Hormuz and that Iranian forces are 'gone,' and reported that large numbers of empty oil tankers are heading to the U.S. Retired Army General Jack Keane urged Trump to further break Iran's control over the strait by stopping Iranian oil-carrying ships from reaching destinations including China. The conflict with Iran has been identified as a contributing factor to surging U.S. consumer prices, with energy and fuel costs implicated.

CENTCOM confirmed on Saturday that U.S. Navy destroyers crossed the Strait of Hormuz, marking the first such transit in several weeks. The crossing was conducted as part of ongoing mine-clearing operations in the waterway.
Following the collapse of U.S.-Iran talks in Pakistan, Trump announced that the U.S. Navy would blockade the Strait of Hormuz and would intercept or interdict vessels that paid tolls to Iran.

U.S. Central Command formally announced a blockade of all maritime traffic entering and exiting Iranian ports, effective April 13 at 10 a.m. ET, pursuant to a presidential directive from Trump. Vessels not bound to or from Iranian ports retain transit rights through the Strait of Hormuz. Oil prices surged following the announcement.

A vessel named Rich Starry, owned by Chinese entity Shanghai Xuanrun Shipping and subject to U.S. sanctions, became the first sanctioned ship to exit the Gulf by transiting the Strait of Hormuz amid the U.S. blockade.
At least four vessels linked to Iran transited the Strait of Hormuz despite the active U.S. naval blockade, with the ships identified through vessel tracking data.
Iran's military threatened to disrupt trade through the Red Sea in response to the ongoing U.S. naval blockade of Iranian ports via the Strait of Hormuz.
Ayman Ghazali, a 41-year-old naturalized U.S. citizen born in Lebanon, drove a vehicle into Temple Israel near Detroit and died by suicide. Israel stated his brother was a Hezbollah commander.
A Syrian man alleged to be a Hezbollah member was arrested in Ecuador and identified as a terrorist threat by the U.S. government.
The FBI formally labeled the March 12 attack on a Michigan synagogue as a Hezbollah-inspired act of terrorism, with the attacker using a truck to ram into the building.
A Hezbollah-inspired terrorist attack occurred on March 12 at Temple Israel synagogue in Michigan, with the attacker dying at the scene.
Cuba experienced an island-wide blackout on Monday due to a complete disconnection of the electrical system during an ongoing energy crisis.
Cuba is experiencing a severe, deepening energy crisis with nationwide blackouts amid broader economic collapse, while the Trump administration exerts significant economic pressure on the country.
Cuba experienced an islandwide blackout amid a deepening energy crisis, prompting Marco Rubio to call for a change in Cuban leadership.

The New York Times reported that the Trump administration is seeking to oust Cuban President Miguel Díaz-Canel; Secretary of State Marco Rubio labeled the story as 'fake' while the Times defended its reporting as 'real and accurate.'
Cuba announced it is preparing to receive its first Russian oil shipment of the year and is operating on natural gas, solar power, and thermoelectric plants while experiencing severe power outages.

A solidarity caravan of 650 delegates representing 33 countries and 120 organizations has begun arriving in Cuba carrying approximately 20 tons of humanitarian aid amid the country's severe energy crisis.
Cuba's national power grid collapsed multiple times in March 2026, leaving the entire island without electricity amid a U.S.-imposed oil blockade and decaying infrastructure.

Cuba experienced a nationwide power grid blackout. Cuban Deputy Foreign Minister Carlos Fernández de Cossío stated the military is preparing for possible military aggression and referenced President Trump's threats regarding a US takeover of Cuba.
Countries sent Cuban doctors home from international medical missions in response to pressure from the Trump administration, reducing a major revenue source for Cuba.
Cuban president Raul Castro is engaged in talks with the US amid an oil blockade, while Trump stated he would be 'honoured' to take over Cuba.
Two humanitarian aid boats traveling to Cuba reported by Mexico's navy are missing, with expected arrival between March 24-25 and no communication received.
Cuba is experiencing an energy crisis while Trump administration officials demand changes from the country.
The Mexican navy found two missing boats carrying aid to Cuba that had departed from Mexican ports. Cuba faces a humanitarian crisis due to a U.S.-imposed fuel embargo.
Two sailboats carrying humanitarian aid that had been reported missing arrived in Havana on Saturday afternoon after traveling from southern Mexico to Cuba.
Cuba is experiencing blackouts as sanctions have deepened, with tensions between Cuba and the U.S. escalating.
The U.S. is allowing a Russian oil tanker to deliver oil to Cuba, representing a relaxation of the existing U.S. blockade. Trump stated he has 'no problem' with the Russian oil tanker reaching Cuba.
Secretary of State Rubio denied in an exclusive Al Jazeera interview that U.S. actions were punitive, instead blaming Cuba for its economic failures amid a fuel crisis.
A sanctioned Russian oil tanker reached Cuba, prompting a White House statement.
Cuba's tourism industry has collapsed, leaving thousands of tourism workers including taxi drivers and tour guides struggling to sustain themselves.
A Russian oil tanker arrived at Cuba's Port of Matanzas while the U.S. maintains an energy blockade against Cuba. Russia indicated it will continue supplying oil to Cuba.
Wartime fuel shortages have spawned panic and robberies in affected areas, with killings reported in relation to fuel shortages.
A fuel tanker rerouted from Cuba is discharging in Venezuela.
Anti-government protests have been growing in Cuba with many regime critics fleeing the island, and no organized opposition exists on the island.
The Cuban government announced the release of 2,010 prisoners based on age and behavior criteria amid ongoing US economic and political pressure.

Cuban President Miguel Diaz-Canel rebuked U.S. threats to take over Cuba, warning that any such effort would lead to 'incalculable' loss of life and invoking the teachings of Fidel Castro. The White House stated the U.S. is already engaged in talks with Cuba, and Democratic lawmakers who returned from Cuba urged negotiations between the two countries. Separately, Egypt has implemented energy conservation measures including 9 p.m. business shutdowns and remote work policies in response to a power crisis.

A Russian diplomat stated that Moscow will not abandon Cuba and will help the country with its energy crisis. Cuban President Miguel Díaz-Canel told NBC News' Meet the Press in his first interview with a U.S. network that he would not step down.

Russian Deputy Foreign Minister Sergei Ryabkov visited Cuban President Miguel Diaz-Canel and praised the 'special relationship' between Russia and Cuba, following a Russian delivery of oil and fuel supplies to Cuba ten days prior. Cuba has endured severe fuel shortages and repeated nationwide blackouts under a months-long fuel blockade imposed by the Trump administration, while Diaz-Canel has vowed to resist U.S. pressure.

Cuban President Miguel Díaz-Canel gave his first interview with a US broadcast network to Kristen Welker of Meet the Press, stating he has no intention of stepping down as the Trump administration increases pressure on Cuba.

In the Meet the Press interview, Díaz-Canel stated he is prepared to 'give my life for the revolution' and said 'if we need to die, we'll die' when referencing resistance to a potential U.S. invasion.
The New York Times published an investigation alleging that Cesar Chavez sexually abused girls and women during his leadership of the farmworkers' rights movement.

California Governor Gavin Newsom said he is still processing the sexual abuse allegations against Cesar Chavez and deferred to lawmakers on renamings, while maintaining Cesar Chavez tributes in his home.

César Chavez has been accused of sexually abusing women and girls associated with the United Farm Workers movement, including allegations from Dolores Huerta, according to a New York Times investigation.
Sexual abuse allegations including from Dolores Huerta have been made against César Chávez; communities across the country are renaming streets and schools named after Chávez in response.
César Chavez has been accused of sexually abusing girls and women, prompting cities and communities to reconsider public honors such as street and school names bearing his name.
A New York Times investigation revealed that Cesar Chavez sexually abused women and girls, resulting in the removal of public references to Chavez across the United States.

A federal judge in Kentucky agreed to toss charges against two former Louisville police officers accused of providing false information on a search warrant related to Breonna Taylor's fatal shooting in 2020.
Sexual assault allegations have been made against Cesar Chavez; residents in Delano farming town are conflicted about how to remember him as March 31 marks his birthday and longtime holiday.

NASA's Artemis II rocket returned to the launch pad at Kennedy Space Center following repairs. The four-person crew will conduct a lunar flyby mission, with launch attempted early next month in a second attempt.
NASA unveiled a $20 billion moon base strategy, with NASA Administrator Jared Isaacman stating the goal is to establish a permanent presence on the moon.
NASA unveiled a $20 billion moon base strategy, with Administrator Jared Isaacman stating 'This time, the goal is to stay' during the announcement.
NASA's Artemis program, aimed at returning humans to the moon, is years behind schedule and billions of dollars over budget.
NASA's Artemis II crew arrived at Kennedy Space Center for a planned 10-day mission set to launch April 1.
The Artemis II crew arrived at Kennedy Space Center for final launch preparations for the first human deep space mission in more than 50 years.
The Artemis II rocket, standing 322 feet tall, was positioned and ready on the pad at Kennedy Space Center on Saturday as the first crewed moonshot in over 50 years.
The Artemis II crew of three Americans and one Canadian is in prelaunch quarantine ahead of a scheduled Wednesday mission, with the astronauts discussing their upcoming mission.
The Artemis II crew is preparing for launch on Wednesday from Kennedy Space Center in Florida with four astronauts on a trip around the moon.
NASA's Artemis II crewed mission to the moon is being actively prepared for launch, marking the first crewed lunar mission in over 50 years.
NASA's Artemis II mission is scheduled to launch Wednesday from Kennedy Space Center, sending four astronauts on a crewed journey around the moon for the first time in over five decades.
Artemis II is ready to fly with Musk and Bezos involved in lunar initiatives alongside NASA.
Harrison Schmitt, 90, walked on the moon during Apollo 17 in 1972, the last human lunar visit; Artemis II is scheduled for Wednesday to return humans to the moon.
Artemis II launched with crew members Reid Wiseman, Christina Koch, Victor Glover, and Canadian Jeremy Hansen. The mission is the first crewed lunar orbit mission since Apollo 17 in 1972.
Artemis II marks the first time humans have headed to the moon since 1972.
Artemis II marks the first time humans have headed to the moon since 1972.
The Orion capsule successfully propelled itself out of Earth's orbit as part of the Artemis II moon mission.
Artemis II crew left Earth's orbit heading toward the Moon after NASA approved the trans-lunar injection burn; this is the first crewed lunar transit since 1972, though the crew will not land.

Artemis II, a NASA mission carrying four astronauts, conducted a 10-day journey around the moon with the crew broadcasting interviews from the Orion capsule. The mission experienced a toilet malfunction onboard the spacecraft.
Artemis II is scheduled to break Apollo 13's distance record during its moon flyby.
Artemis II experienced an approximately 40-minute communication blackout during the lunar flyby.

The Artemis II crew broke the distance record previously held by Apollo 13, traveling farther from Earth than any humans in history. The crew passed behind the far side of the moon during a planned communications blackout before contact was reestablished, and they are now returning to Earth. President Trump called the crew to congratulate them, calling them 'modern-day pioneers.'

The four-person Artemis II crew completed a lunar flyby, traveling farther into space than any humans in history before beginning their return to Earth. The crew were the first humans to lay eyes on several craters on the far side of the moon and captured images including an Earthrise, an Earthset, and a solar eclipse. Commander Reid Wiseman proposed naming one of the newly observed craters after his late wife Carroll Wiseman, who died of cancer in 2020. NASA and the White House released imagery from the mission, and astronaut Christina Koch shared photos on social media; the crew also created a recreation of the 'Full House' opening credits titled 'Full Capsule.'

The Artemis II crew completed a historic loop around the moon's far side and is now heading back to Earth. The astronauts became the first humans to see parts of the moon's far side with the naked eye, and NASA released mission photos including a historic 'Earthset' image showing Earth as seen from the moon's far side.

The Artemis II crew completed a lunar flyby aboard the Orion spacecraft and is now heading back to Earth, with splashdown expected Friday evening. The four astronauts captured and shared photographs of the moon, including its far side, during the mission.

The Artemis II mission's four-person crew is returning to Earth for splashdown off the coast of San Diego, with flight controllers at Johnson Space Center in Houston preparing for reentry. The crew held an in-flight press conference before reentry and captured photographs and data from their lunar mission. Separately, a moon crater was named after one astronaut's wife, and the astronaut publicly reacted to the naming.

NASA's Artemis II crew completed a historic trip to and around the Moon and is scheduled to splashdown off the Southern California coast on Friday. During the mission, astronauts photographed a large lunar crater formation resembling a smiling face, conducted workouts using a compact flywheel device, and participated in a small-scale medical experiment described as having potential to reshape medicine.

The four-person Artemis II crew spent their last full day in space on Thursday after a 10-day lunar flyby mission aboard the Orion spacecraft, with splashdown scheduled for Friday off the California coast. The mission included several scientific experiments, among them one involving the astronauts' bone marrow. The launch drew widespread public attention, including an Orlando mother whose photograph of her sons watching the launch from their front yard was seen by millions.

NASA's Artemis II mission, carrying four astronauts, completed a historic 10-day crewed journey around the far side of the moon. The crew is scheduled to return to Earth via splashdown in the Pacific Ocean on Friday.

The Artemis II crew of four astronauts — Commander Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover, Christina Koch, and Canadian astronaut Jeremy Hansen — splashed down in the Pacific Ocean on Friday evening after completing NASA's first crewed lunar mission in more than 50 years. Flying aboard the Orion capsule, the crew traveled farther from Earth than any humans in history, circumnavigating the moon and returning safely using parachutes during descent before being airlifted by helicopter.

The Artemis II four-person crew successfully splashed down in the Pacific Ocean on Friday following a 10-day mission around the moon. The crew was recovered aboard a U.S. Navy ship after splashdown. The mission was described as historic, and the crew subsequently spoke publicly about the mission.
At their first public appearance on Saturday following Friday's splashdown, the Artemis II crew described their bond using the phrase 'bonded forever.'

Iran rejected President Trump's 15-point peace plan and countered with its own demands, including control over the Strait of Hormuz, collection of shipping fees, and keeping its missile program without limitations.
The U.S. experienced its weakest Treasury auctions in over three years as investor anxiety around the Iran conflict grew.
One month into the Iran conflict, public polling shows disapproval over the war and petrol prices, while Republicans and Democrats remain entrenched with no coordinated action on the situation.

Iran's Parliamentary Speaker warned U.S. troops would face fierce resistance if deployed, while Pakistan hosted diplomatic talks with Saudi Arabia, Turkey, and Egypt aimed at de-escalation. The U.S.-Iran conflict has been ongoing for approximately one month, with the Pentagon reportedly preparing ground force options and multiple Republican lawmakers not ruling out ground troop deployment.
The International Atomic Energy Agency confirmed on Sunday that Iran's Khondab heavy water plant is offline after suffering severe damage from an Israeli strike on Friday.
President Trump stated that regime change in Iran has been accomplished and that new Iranian leaders are making concessions; more U.S. troops arrived in the Middle East.
Trump told reporters Tuesday he would be leaving Iran "very soon"; the war is in its fifth week.
Trump and Israeli Prime Minister Netanyahu started a war against Iran together, though they have divergent priorities regarding the war's outcome.
Wartime fuel shortages in Asia have sparked panic, robberies, and killings in affected areas.

A U.S. F-15 fighter jet was shot down by a shoulder-fired missile in Iran, and a Pentagon press briefing scheduled for Tuesday with Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth and Joint Chiefs Chair Gen. Dan Caine was canceled. Gas prices rose for the ninth consecutive day, reaching a national average of $4.14 per gallon — the highest level since 2022. Some Republican lawmakers expressed growing wariness about the military conflict as it entered its sixth week during midterm election season. Rep. Yassamin Ansari announced she would introduce articles of impeachment against Defense Secretary Hegseth related to planned strikes on Iranian civilian infrastructure. Trump threatened to jail reporters who refused to reveal sources connected to coverage of Iran's strike on a U.S. fighter jet.

President Trump posted on Truth Social that Iran's 'whole civilization will die tonight, never to be brought back again' ahead of an 8 p.m. Tuesday deadline for Tehran to act. The White House responded to questions about potential nuclear weapon use in Iran, denying such a plan. Over 30 congressional Democrats called on the Cabinet to remove Trump from office over his threats to target civilian infrastructure, while House Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries called on Republicans to 'stop the madness' and Sen. Richard Blumenthal warned that 'bombing alone' cannot achieve Trump's objectives. Iranian hackers also escalated targeting of U.S. critical infrastructure following the onset of the conflict.

President Trump on Tuesday threatened that 'a whole civilization will die tonight' and set an 8 p.m. deadline for Iran to reach a peace deal, while also threatening to destroy Tehran's bridges and power plants. The UN Secretary-General stated he was 'deeply troubled' by the statements, and Pope Leo XIV, the first American pontiff, called Trump's threat 'truly unacceptable' outside his residence in Castel Gandolfo. Congressional Democrats, including former House Speaker Nancy Pelosi, called for Trump's removal via the 25th Amendment, while dozens of Democrats demanded he be removed from office. Some Republicans expressed unease, with Sen. Lisa Murkowski urging de-escalation and Rep. Nathaniel Moran stating he does not support destroying a 'whole civilization,' though most GOP lawmakers stayed largely silent. Rep. Jason Crow urged U.S. service members to follow the law if directed to strike Iranian civilian targets, characterizing such strikes as war crimes, an assessment echoed by a New York Times analysis. Separately, American officials reported that Iran-linked hackers were disrupting systems tied to critical U.S. infrastructure.

Iran-linked hackers were responsible for recent disruptions to internet access for companies tied to US oil, gas, and water infrastructure, according to an FBI report published Tuesday. The report warned of an increased effort by hackers to take over Programmable Logic Controller systems. The disclosures came amid the broader US-Iran military escalation that culminated in a ceasefire following Trump's ultimatum tied to the Strait of Hormuz dispute.

More than 50 Democratic lawmakers invoked the 25th Amendment to call for President Trump's removal after Trump posted a social media threat warning that 'a whole civilization will die tonight' unless Iran met his terms by a Tuesday deadline. The White House sent an email to staff last month warning against using nonpublic information to trade on financial markets, following concerns raised by well-timed bets on oil markets and prediction markets tied to the Iran war. Democrats at a New York convention rallied around opposition to Trump's attack on Iran, with several members mulling presidential bids, while Sen. John Fetterman broke with his party to criticize increasing Democratic criticism of Israel over its joint operations with the U.S. against Iran.

The Senate held its fourth war powers vote to limit Trump's authority in Iran on Wednesday, but the resolution again failed to pass. Separately, Trump publicly criticized Pope Leo XIV on social media over the pope's statements regarding the Iran conflict, escalating a feud between the two figures.
Kim Jong Un personally observed a North Korean high-thrust missile engine test designed for ICBMs capable of targeting the US mainland, as reported by North Korean state media.

North Korea conducted a solid-fuel rocket engine test overseen by Kim Jong Un, claiming the missile is capable of targeting the U.S. mainland as part of efforts to develop long-range strike capabilities.

North Korea conducted a missile test on Sunday for a solid-fuel engine, claiming it can reach the U.S. mainland with a maximum thrust of 2,500 kilonewtons, a significant increase from 1,970 kilonewtons in a September test.
South Korea assessed that North Korea is distancing itself from Iran, possibly to leave open the possibility of talks with the United States.
South Korea reports North Korea is keeping Iran at arm's length and is wary in its approach to Iran; North Korea is seeking to renew diplomatic dialogue with the United States.

North Korea fired ballistic missiles toward the East Sea, with South Korea's military confirming the launches amid rising tensions on the Korean Peninsula. North Korea also dismissed South Korea's hopes for improved diplomatic ties.

North Korea conducted a weapons testing spree this week involving multiple new systems, including ballistic missiles armed with cluster-bomb warheads, as part of efforts to expand its nuclear-capable forces.

American journalist Shelly Kittleson, a contributor to Al-Monitor, was kidnapped in Baghdad, Iraq by the Iranian-backed Iraqi militia Kataib Hezbollah. She was released after approximately one week in captivity, with the condition that she leave Iraq immediately. Iraqi officials stated she was released in exchange for the release of militia members held by Iraqi authorities.

Secretary of State Marco Rubio announced the release of American journalist Shelly Kittleson, who had been kidnapped and held hostage in Iraq for just over a week by an Iran-backed militia. Efforts are underway to facilitate her safe departure from Iraq.
